settings.title: Editorial Feature for Harvest Quarterly
settings.summary: We photographed a ten-page farm-to-table feature for a regional food magazine.
settings.description:
Harvest Quarterly was running a feature on small upstate farms and wanted images that matched the writing rather than generic stock. The piece followed real growers through a season, and stock imagery would have undercut the honesty the editors were after.

The brief

The editors needed enough range to carry ten pages, from wide establishing shots of the land down to close detail of hands and produce, all reading as one cohesive feature. The images also had to leave room for the cover and contents page if the right frame turned up.

On location

We spent three days on location across four farms, shooting the growers at work, the fields through the day, and the kitchen prep that closes the farm-to-table loop. We worked entirely in natural light to keep the feel honest and to match the unhurried tone of the writing.

  • Environmental portraits of the growers
  • Wide field and landscape frames for the openers
  • Detail shots of produce and kitchen prep
  • Cover and contents candidates pulled from the same set

Four farms in three days meant chasing the light and the work as it actually happened. We shot around each farm's real schedule, harvest in the morning, prep later, rather than staging it, which is what gave the feature its lived-in feel.

The result

The set carried a ten-page spread, and two of the frames ran as the cover and contents image for the issue. Because everything came from the same three days and the same natural light, the feature held together from the cover through the last page.
